title: Height Balanced Binary Tree

content.problemStatement:
Given a connected binary tree with **N** nodes, determine if it is height-balanced. (**Note:** A binary tree is height-balanced if the depth of the two subtrees of every node never differs by more than one.)

For example, the following binary tree is height balanced:




Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the binary tree.
Next N−1N - 1N−1 lines contain three space separated characters pi,ci,Rip_i, c_i, R_ipi​,ci​,Ri​, describing the tree with edge informations - pip_ipi​ is an integer denoting the parent node of the iiith edge, cic_ici​ is child node, and the letter RiR_iRi​ denotes whether the child is left child or right child, it's value is `L` if cic_ici​ is left child of pip_ipi​, else `R`.


Output Format
Output on the single line, `YES` if the tree is height balanced, else `NO`.
constraints: All (pi,ci) pairs are distinct and form a valid binary tree
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N — the number of nodes in the binary tree. The next N‑1 lines each contain three space‑separated values: pi ci Ri, where pi is the parent node, ci is the child node and Ri is either 'L' (ci is the left child of pi) or 'R' (ci is the right child of pi).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing "YES" if the given binary tree is height‑balanced, otherwise print "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Root 1 has two children that are leaves, so the tree is perfectly balanced.
The tree is a left‑skewed chain; the height difference exceeds one at several nodes, so it is not height‑balanced.
A single node tree is trivially height‑balanced.

solution.approach: A binary tree is height‑balanced if for every node the heights of its left and right sub‑trees differ by at most one. We can compute the height of each subtree using a post‑order DFS. While returning from recursion we also check the balance condition; if any subtree is unbalanced we propagate a sentinel value (e.g., -1). The algorithm visits each node once, giving O(N) time per test case and O(N) auxiliary space for the recursion stack and the child arrays.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) for all test cases combined.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) per test case for the left, right and isChild arrays plus recursion stack.
